abstract: This paper examines how institutional roles and ideological positions shape the online sharing of misinformation by elected officials in the United States. The authors investigate whether certain political contexts function as checks that constrain or enable the spread of low-credibility information by politicians. Published in Political Communication, the study contributes to understanding elite-driven misinformation dynamics on social media.
key_claims: Elected officials' propensity to share misinformation online is conditioned by institutional and ideological factors
Certain institutional positions and ideological orientations act as checks that moderate misinformation-sharing behavior among politicians
methods: Empirical analysis of online sharing behavior by elected officials, focused on misinformation or low-credibility content
Comparison across institutional roles and ideological positions to identify moderating factors
findings: Institutional and ideological context systematically shape patterns of misinformation sharing among elected officials (specific empirical details not available from abstract alone)
contributions: Advances scholarship on elite-driven political misinformation by identifying institutional and ideological conditions that constrain or amplify its diffusion
Contributes to political communication research on the role of officeholders in shaping the online information environment
framing: The paper situates itself within political communication research on misinformation, elite cues, and the role of elected officials in shaping online discourse. It engages debates about how institutional design and partisan ideology condition democratic accountability in digital information ecosystems.
